comment_text.sn19.12:1.2: Brahmins were obsessed with purity of food.
comment_text.sn19.12:1.3: Doniya (“trough”) is used for such things as a feeding trough for dogs or pigs (MN 93:11.7), or a casket for a corpse (DN 16:6.17.6).

root_text.sn19.12:1.1: “Idhāhaṁ, āvuso, gijjhakūṭā pabbatā orohanto addasaṁ purisaṁ gūthakūpe nimuggaṁ ubhohi hatthehi gūthaṁ khādantaṁ …pe…
root_text.sn19.12:1.2: eso, bhikkhave, satto imasmiṁyeva rājagahe duṭṭhabrāhmaṇo ahosi.
root_text.sn19.12:1.3: So kassapassa sammāsambuddhassa pāvacane bhikkhusaṅghaṁ bhattena nimantetvā doṇiyo gūthassa pūrāpetvā etadavoca—
root_text.sn19.12:1.4: aho bhonto yāvadatthaṁ bhuñjantu ceva harantu cā’ti …pe….

translation_text.sn19.12:1.1: “Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a man sunk in a sewer, eating dung with both hands. …” …
translation_text.sn19.12:1.2: “That being used to be a nasty brahmin right here in Rājagaha.
translation_text.sn19.12:1.3: In the time of the Buddha Kassapa’s dispensation he invited the Saṅgha of mendicants for a meal. He filled a trough with dung and said:
translation_text.sn19.12:1.4: ‘My good men, eat as much as you like, and take what’s left.’ …”

variant_text.sn19.12:1.3: doṇiyo → doṇiyā (sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed, mr)
